query: clinical features and diagnostics of myxedema coma

retrieved_docs.title: Myxedema -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: An endocrinology consult is warranted once the diagnosis of myxedema coma is suspected.

retrieved_docs.title: Myxedema -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: A high index of clinician suspicion for myxedema coma is important. As discussed above, patients who present will most likely be women in the winter months with a history of thyroid disorders and a precipitating illness. The two most common findings will be altered mental status and hypothermia, along with common findings of hypothyroidism. Other common signs include hyponatremia, hypotension, bradycardia, and hypoventilation. Laboratory results in primary hypothyroidism will show severely low or undetected low serum total T4, free T4, free T3, and elevated TSH. The underlying etiology for most patients is a primary thyroid failure, but secondary, tertiary, and sick euthyroid should be in the differential diagnosis. [48] In euthyroid sick syndrome, TSH will not be as elevated as expected. In a small percentage of patients, central hypothyroidism should be a consideration. In these patients, TSH may be inappropriately low. The method to distinguish central versus primary hypothyroidism is that the associated pituitary hormones will decrease as well. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Myxedema -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Myxedema coma is a rare and extreme complication of hypothyroidism with multiple organ abnormalities associated with altered sensorium, it can be fatal. Hypothyroid patients exhibit multiple physiologic alterations to compensate for the deficiency of thyroid hormone. Factors like infection can overwhelm these homeostatic mechanisms and can lead to myxedema coma. Myxedema coma can be an initial presentation of any form of hypothyroidism, irrespective of the cause. [1] Even with early diagnosis and treatment of myxedema coma, the mortality rate is variable, with some reports as high as 60% and others as low as 20 to 25% even with intensive care.

retrieved_docs.title: Myxedema -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Differential diagnosis has its basis on signs and symptoms; for example, the differential diagnosis of altered mental status [67] or coma would include: Sepsis Shock Stroke Drug overdose Diabetic ketoacidosis Seizure Hypothermia [67]

retrieved_docs.title: Myxedema -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Myxedema coma is a rare fatal condition resulting from long-standing hypothyroidism with loss of the adaptive mechanism to maintain homeostasis. Hypothyroidism due to any cause, including autoimmune disease, iodine deficiency, congenital abnormalities, or medications like lithium and amiodarone, can precipitate myxedema coma if left untreated. Even with early diagnosis and treatment of myxedema coma, the mortality rate is variable, with some reports as high as 60% and others as low as 20 to 25% in the presence of advanced intensive support care. Early recognition, a thorough history, physical exam, and early treatment are paramount in managing myxedema coma. This activity reviews the cause, pathophysiology, and presentation of myxedema and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in management.

retrieved_docs.title: Myxedema -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Myxedema coma is a potentially fatal complication, and prompt diagnosis is an important factor in reducing mortality. [5] Identifying a precipitating cause is imperative. It is worth noting that a patient usually presents with hypothermia and is not febrile. [22] However, an investigation for an infectious cause is warranted, particularly if the patient is minimally hypothermic as it may be masking the underlying infection. [15] Particular drugs such as lithium and amiodarone have multiple case reports associating their use and precipitation of myxedema coma given their mechanism of action on the inhibition of thyroid hormone release. [23] Lithium also inhibits cAMP production in response to TSH. Patients on lithium also have been shown to have an exaggerated TSH response to TRH. [24] Amiodarone inhibits thyroid hormone entry into peripheral tissues, inhibits 5’-deiodinase, converts T4 to T3, and releases excess iodine. [25] Other precipitating factors such as hypoglycemia, hypoxemia, and hypercapnia may also be secondary to myxedema coma itself.

retrieved_docs.title: Myxedema -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the history and physical exam of a patient with myxedema coma. Identify the etiology of myxedema coma. Outline the management options available for myxedema coma. Review the importance of an interprofessional team for improving outcomes of myxedema coma. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Myxedema -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis for myxedema coma is difficult to establish due to the small number of cases reported. The mortality rate is variable, with some reports as high as 60% and others as low as 20 to 25% in the presence of advanced intensive support care. [68] Poor prognosis is likely related to advanced age, bradycardia, and persistent hypothermia. [54]

retrieved_docs.title: Myxedema -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Disrupted homeostatic mechanisms in hypothyroid patients lead to myxedema coma. There are many Precipitating factors. The most important are:

retrieved_docs.title: Myxedema -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Myxedema is commonly used often in the context of hypothyroidism and myxedema coma, it also means edema of the skin and soft tissue in hypothyroidism. [2] Myxedema coma is a misnomer. Neither the patients have classic nonpitting edema, nor are they in a coma. The most salient feature of myxedema coma is deteriorating mentation.

retrieved_docs.title: Myxedema -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: A high index of suspicion, early recognition, admission to intensive care units, and treatment with intravenous levothyroxine and hydrocortisone are paramount in the management of myxedema coma. [3] History of any thyroid dysfunction, thyroid medication, adherence with thyroid medication, thyroid surgery, and history of any drugs that may affect thyroid function require assessment in any patient suspected myxedema coma. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Myxedema -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Myxedema coma is often a fatal complication, and prompt diagnosis is an important factor in reducing mortality. [5] This disorder is best managed by an interprofessional team to enable prompt diagnosis and management. Identifying a precipitating cause is imperative. [22] Effective treatment calls for a team-based and patient-centered approach. An endocrinology consult is necessary on clinical suspicion. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Hypercarbia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Further evaluation should be driven by history and physical exam findings. A basic metabolic panel is useful to assess for metabolic derangements and better characterize the serum bicarbonate. A urine drug screen is useful if an overdose is suspected. Work up for sepsis and fever should include complete blood count, chest imaging, and blood and sputum cultures. Thyroid function is important to assess as different disease states can contribute to hypercarbia (increased production in thyrotoxicosis, decreased clearance in myxedema coma). [13] Head imaging is also critical in evaluation, as an acute stroke can cause CNS depression and subsequent hypoventilation.

retrieved_docs.title: Myxedema -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Patients most commonly present for emergency services with altered mental status and hypothermia, below 35.5°C (95.9° F). The lower the body temperature, the worst is the prognosis. [45] The absence of mild diastolic hypertension in severely hypothyroid patients is a warning sign of impending myxedema coma. [46] Due to the presence of altered mental status, a definitive history may be difficult to obtain. Important historical features include any thyroid dysfunction, the dosage of thyroid medication, adherence with thyroid medication, thyroid surgery, and history of any drugs that may affect thyroid function. A quick and detailed physical examination is a must in the initial assessment of myxedema coma. A physical exam can help confirm the suspicion of thyroid history by findings of no palpable thyroid tissue, goiter, sparse hair, non-pitting edema, a surgical scar on the neck, or dry skin. Some elderly patients have atypical presentations, such as decreased mobility. [47]

retrieved_docs.title: Case Study: 60-Year-Old Female Presenting With Shortness of Breath (Archived) -- Management -- Neurologic
retrieved_docs.content: The patient’s mental status rapidly worsened despite therapy. In the setting of her hypothyroidism history, this may be myxedema coma or due to the involvement of another organ system. The thyroid supplementation medications and hydrocortisone were continued. A CT head without contrast was normal.

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity-Hypoventilation Syndrome -- Differential Diagnosis -- Myxedema
retrieved_docs.content: Severely low levels of circulating free thyroid hormones can lead to respiratory insufficiency and hypercapnic respiratory failure. Affected individuals typically present with additional features such as hypothermia, bradycardia, and delayed tendon reflexes. In severe cases, patients may exhibit hemodynamic instability and neurological deficits, including coma.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1756
retrieved_docs.content: The category “DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES” encompasses a spectrum of disorders and clinical manifestations that arise from dysfunction of the pancreatic endocrine system. It includes “ارتفاع السكر في الدم” (hyperglycemia), which represents elevated blood‑glucose levels and is a primary feature of diabetes. “غيبوبة فرط الأسمولية السكري” (diabetic hyperosmolar coma) is a severe, acute complication of uncontrolled hyperglycemia characterized by extreme dehydration and altered mental status. “مرض السكري دون مضاعفات” (diabetes mellitus without complications) denotes diabetes in its uncomplicated form, serving as the baseline condition from which other sequelae may develop. Among the chronic complications, “اعتلال عصبي سكري” (diabetic neuropathy) reflects nerve damage caused by prolonged hyperglycemia, leading to sensory and motor deficits. “ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي” (progressive hemifacial atrophy) is listed as a possible extra‑pancreatic manifestation that can be associated with underlying endocrine pancreatic disease. Metabolic dysregulation is further illustrated by “فرط أنسولين الدم” (hyperinsulinemia), an excess of circulating insulin that may precede or accompany insulin resistance. Finally, “ورم غلوكاغوني” (glucagonoma) represents a rare pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor that secretes excess glucagon, producing a distinct clinical syndrome. Together, these entities illustrate the diverse acute, chronic, and neoplastic conditions linked to diseases of the endocrine pancreas, highlighting the importance of comprehensive assessment and management of both glycemic control and associated complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 1242
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to and may be considered synonymous with the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions. Within this group of nonspecific clinical manifestations, the specific finding of الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse) is recognized as a child element, illustrating that severe cardiovascular failure can be one of the many possible presentations encompassed by ill‑defined symptom complexes. This relationship underscores the importance of viewing individual symptoms as part of a larger, sometimes vague, clinical picture when assessing patients with ambiguous or poorly characterized disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1973
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to, and may be considered synonymous with, the signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, reflecting the overlap between patient‑reported experiences and observable clinical manifestations in poorly characterized disorders. In this framework, diseases and injuries are placed under the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that many inadequately defined pathologies present with nonspecific clinical findings that are captured by this umbrella term. Together, these relationships underscore the interconnected nature of clinical presentations (symptoms) and the underlying, often vague, disease or injury processes, highlighting the importance of comprehensive evaluation when managing patients with ill‑defined conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2675
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to, and may be considered synonymous with, the signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, reflecting an overlap in terminology where both terms describe the observable manifestations and patient‑reported experiences associated with poorly characterized medical disorders. This conceptual equivalence highlights the importance of recognizing that “symptoms” and “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” often refer to the same clinical features, facilitating clearer communication in diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1545
retrieved_docs.content: The category “DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES” encompasses a spectrum of pancreatic and metabolic disorders. It includes “مرض السكري دون مضاعفات” (diabetes mellitus without complications) as the baseline form of the disease, as well as “مرض السكري نوع 2” (type 2 diabetes), which represents the most common adult‑onset variant characterized by insulin resistance. Severe acute metabolic decompensation can manifest as “غيبوبة فرط الأسمولية السكري” (diabetic hyperosmolar coma), a life‑threatening state of extreme hyperglycemia and dehydration. Chronic complications of diabetes are reflected in “اعتلال عصبي سكري” (diabetic neuropathy), a progressive nerve damage that can cause pain, sensory loss, and autonomic dysfunction. Hormonal dysregulation associated with pancreatic endocrine tumors is represented by “فرط أنسولين الدم” (hyperinsulinemia), often due to insulin‑secreting neoplasms, and “ورم غلوكاغوني” (glucagonoma), a rare tumor that overproduces glucagon leading to characteristic skin and metabolic findings. Additionally, the list includes “ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي” (progressive facial hemi‑atrophy), a condition that, while not a classic diabetic complication, may be observed in the broader context of endocrine pancreatic disease. Together, these entities illustrate the diverse clinical presentations—from uncomplicated hyperglycemia to acute metabolic crises, chronic neuropathic damage, and endocrine neoplasms—associated with diseases of the endocrine pancreas, underscoring the importance of comprehensive evaluation and management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: Elevated plasma osmolality is clinically associated with hyperglycemia and with abnormal psychological states, and it commonly co‑occurs with low blood volume. This interrelationship indicates that patients who present with high plasma osmolality may also exhibit elevated blood glucose levels, mental status disturbances, and hypovolemia, all of which are important considerations for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1771
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to and may be considered synonymous with the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions. In this hierarchical framework, diseases and injuries are classified as having the parent category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that they are understood through the manifestation of such nonspecific signs. A specific example of a child element within this category is الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse), which represents a distinct clinical manifestation that can arise from ill‑defined conditions. Together, these relationships highlight that the identification of symptoms and signs, including serious events like circulatory collapse, is essential for recognizing and managing diseases and injuries that present without clear, well‑defined diagnostic criteria.
